teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** An actionable misrepresentation is a false statement of existing fact made by one contracting party to the other, which induces the other to enter the contract. The statement must be one of fact — not mere opinion, not future intention (unless the representor had no intention of performing), and not a pure statement of law (though the fact/law distinction is eroding: *Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Lincoln City Council* [1999]).

**The Reliance Principle — Edgington v Fitzmaurice [1885] 29 Ch D 459:** The representee need not have relied exclusively on the misrepresentation. It is sufficient that the false statement was 'a' material inducement — one of the reasons, not necessarily the sole or predominant reason. In Edgington, a company issued a prospectus falsely stating it intended to use loan funds to expand the business (it actually intended to pay off debts). Investors lent money partly because of the prospectus and partly because they mistakenly believed they would receive a charge over company assets. Despite this mixed motive, the claim succeeded: the false statement was a material factor.

**Materiality:** A statement is material if a reasonable person would have been influenced. But even if a reasonable person would not have been influenced, if the representee was in fact induced, the misrepresentation may still be actionable — subject to the representor's knowledge of the representee's idiosyncrasies.

**Key Exclusions:** (1) Mere 'puffs' or sales talk; (2) Statements of opinion (unless not genuinely held, or impliedly misstating underlying facts — *Smith v Land & House Property Corp* [1884] 28 Ch D 7); (3) Statements of future intention (unless there was no intention to perform — *Edgington*); (4) Pure statements of law (though the distinction is criticised).

**In short:** A false statement of fact that materially contributes to the representee's decision to contract — even alongside other motives — is an actionable misrepresentation.
oscoaReferences: Smith v Land & House Property Corp (1884) 28 Ch D 7
Kleinwort Benson Ltd v Lincoln City Council [1999] 2 AC 349

question: _________ v Fitzmaurice [1885] 29 Ch D 459 established that a misrepresentation need only be 'a' material inducement — the representee need not have relied on it exclusively.
feedback: Correct. *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] 29 Ch D 459 is the leading authority on mixed motives. The investor lent money partly due to the false prospectus and partly because of a mistaken belief about a charge. The claim succeeded because the misrepresentation was one material factor among several.

question: Arrange the elements required to establish an actionable misrepresentation in their logical order:
answer: A false statement was made → The statement was one of existing fact → The statement was made by one contracting party to the other → The statement induced the representee to enter the contract → The statement was material or was actually relied upon

options.text: A false statement was made
The statement was one of existing fact (not opinion, not future intention)
The statement was made by one contracting party to the other
The statement induced the representee to enter the contract
The statement was material or was actually relied upon

feedback: Correct. These are the five core elements. Note that materiality and inducement are related but distinct: materiality asks what a reasonable person would do; inducement asks what this particular representee did. Under *Edgington* [1885], the inducement need not be exclusive.

question: Priya reads a company prospectus stating 'The company owns its factory premises and all machinery outright.' Based partly on this and partly on her own market research, she invests £75,000. In fact, the factory and machinery are leased. Priya admits she would 'probably have still invested' even without the prospectus statement, but she considered it a 'reassuring factor.' Under *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885], what is the legal position?

options.text: Priya cannot claim — she admits she would probably have invested anyway, so the misrepresentation was not decisive
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Edgington* [1885] expressly rejects the requirement of exclusive reliance. The misrepresentation need only be 'a' material inducement, not 'the' inducement.

options.text: Priya can claim — it is sufficient that the false statement was 'a' material inducement; she need not prove it was the sole or predominant reason
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885], the misrepresentation need only be one of the factors that induced the contract. Mixed motives do not defeat the claim, provided the misrepresentation materially contributed.

options.text: Priya cannot claim because she relied on her own market research — self-induced reliance defeats the claim
options.feedback: Incorrect. While a representee who relies entirely on their own inquiries rather than the representation may fail, Priya relied partly on the prospectus and partly on her own research. That is sufficient under *Edgington*.

options.text: Priya can claim only if she rescinds the entire investment contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Rescission is one remedy, but damages are also available under the Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.2(1). The remedy does not determine whether there was a misrepresentation.

feedback: Under *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] 29 Ch D 459, the false statement need only be 'a' material inducement. The investor in Edgington had mixed motives (the false prospectus and a mistaken belief about security) — yet he still succeeded. Priya's position mirrors this: the prospectus statement was a material factor she considered reassuring. She need not prove it was the exclusive or even predominant reason.

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'A false statement made during contractual negotiations will always entitle the innocent party to rescind the contract. The representee must prove that the false statement was the only reason they entered the contract. If the representee had any other motive, however minor, the misrepresentation claim must fail.'
answer: Two errors: (1) A misrepresentation need not be the sole or exclusive reason for entry into the contract — under *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885], it is sufficient that it was 'a' material inducement. (2) Not all false statements give an automatic right to rescind — there are bars to rescission (affirmation, lapse of time, third party rights, restitutio in integrum impossible).
feedback: Found them. *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] 29 Ch D 459 clearly establishes that mixed motives do not defeat a claim. The misrepresentation must be an inducement, but it need not be the exclusive one. Separately, rescission is an equitable remedy subject to bars — it is not automatic.

question: Which TWO of the following are required for a statement to be an actionable misrepresentation under English law?

options.text: The statement must be one of existing fact (not mere opinion, not future intention)
options.feedback: Correct. An actionable misrepresentation requires a false statement of existing fact. Opinions and statements of future intention are generally excluded, though there are important exceptions.

options.text: The misrepresentation must be the sole or predominant reason the representee entered the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] expressly held that the misrepresentation need only be 'a' material inducement. Mixed motives do not defeat the claim.

options.text: The statement must be made by one contracting party to the other and must induce entry into the contract
options.feedback: Correct. The misrepresentation must be made between the contracting parties and must have induced the representee to enter the contract. A statement made to a third party, or one that does not induce, is not actionable.

options.text: The representor must have intended to deceive the representee
options.feedback: Incorrect. Intention to deceive is relevant only to fraudulent misrepresentation. Negligent and innocent misrepresentations are actionable without any intention to deceive.

options.text: The statement must be made in writing
options.feedback: Incorrect. A misrepresentation can be oral, written, or by conduct. There is no requirement of writing.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). A misrepresentation requires a false statement of existing fact, made between the contracting parties, that induces entry into the contract. It need not be the sole inducement (*Edgington* [1885]), need not be intentional (negligent and innocent misrepresentations exist), and can be oral or written.

question: In Bisset v __________ [1927] AC 177, the Privy Council held that a vendor's statement about the grazing capacity of land was a statement of opinion, not fact — and therefore not an actionable misrepresentation.
feedback: Correct. *Bisset v Wilkinson* [1927] AC 177 — the vendor honestly believed the land could support 2,000 sheep. The statement was an opinion, not a representation of fact. The fact/opinion distinction is foundational: a statement of opinion is actionable only if the representor did not genuinely hold it.

question: Match each concept to its correct definition in the law of misrepresentation:
answer: False statement of existing fact=A representation about a past or present state of affairs that can be objectively verified as true or false; Statement of opinion=A belief or judgment, generally not actionable unless not genuinely held; Statement of future intention=A representation about future conduct, actionable only if the representor had no intention of performing at the time; Material inducement=A factor that contributes to the representee's decision to enter the contract, even if not the sole reason

options.text: A representation about a past or present state of affairs that can be objectively verified as true or false
A belief or judgment, generally not actionable unless not genuinely held
A representation about future conduct, actionable only if the representor had no intention of performing at the time
A factor that contributes to the representee's decision to enter the contract, even if not the sole reason

feedback: Well matched. These four concepts form the foundation of the law of misrepresentation. The critical distinction is between fact (actionable) and opinion or future intention (generally not actionable, subject to important exceptions). Material inducement is the key test for causation under *Edgington* [1885].

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Misrepresentations are categorised into three types — fraudulent, negligent, and innocent — each with distinct definitions, burdens of proof, and remedies. A fourth category, common law negligent misstatement, operates alongside the statutory framework.

**1. Fraudulent Misrepresentation — Derry v Peek [1889] 14 App Cas 337:** A fraudulent misrepresentation is made 'knowingly, or without belief in its truth, or recklessly, careless whether it be true or false.' The burden is on the representee to prove fraud (a high bar). Damages are awarded in the tort of deceit — all direct loss flowing from the misrepresentation, regardless of foreseeability. Remoteness is no defence.

**2. Negligent Misrepresentation (Statutory) — s.2(1) Misrepresentation Act 1967:** Once a misrepresentation is proved, the burden shifts — the representor must prove they had reasonable grounds to believe, and did believe up to the time of the contract, that the facts represented were true. This reverse burden makes s.2(1) a powerful tool: it is usually easier to establish than common law negligence. Damages are measured as if the representation were fraudulent (*Royscot Trust Ltd v Rogerson* [1991] 2 QB 297, though controversial).

**3. Innocent Misrepresentation — s.2(2) Misrepresentation Act 1967:** Where the misrepresentation was neither fraudulent nor negligent, the court has discretion to award damages in lieu of rescission. This operates as a safety net where rescission is unavailable or disproportionate.

**4. Common Law Negligent Misstatement — Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465:** A duty of care can arise independently of contract where a 'special relationship' exists — essentially, one party possesses special skill or knowledge, assumes responsibility, and the other reasonably relies. Heller was not liable because of a disclaimer, but the principle was established.

**In short:** Fraud requires proof of dishonesty; s.2(1) reverses the burden; s.2(2) provides equitable discretion; and Hedley Byrne creates a common law alternative where a special relationship exists.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
Royscot Trust Ltd v Rogerson [1991] 2 QB 297

question: _________ v Peek [1889] 14 App Cas 337 provides the classic definition of fraudulent misrepresentation: a statement made 'knowingly, or without belief in its truth, or recklessly, careless whether it be true or false.'
feedback: Correct. *Derry v Peek* [1889] 14 App Cas 337 established the test for fraudulent misrepresentation. The House of Lords distinguished fraud from mere carelessness — recklessness as to truth falls within fraud, but honest belief (even if unreasonable) does not.

question: Under s.______(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967, once a misrepresentation is proved, the burden shifts to the representor to prove they had reasonable grounds to believe the facts represented were true.
feedback: Correct. Section 2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 reverses the burden of proof. The representee need only prove a misrepresentation was made; the representor must then prove reasonable grounds for belief. This makes s.2(1) a valuable alternative to a claim in the tort of deceit.

question: Match each type of misrepresentation to its correct legal test or description:
answer: Fraudulent=Made knowingly, without belief in its truth, or recklessly — Derry v Peek [1889]; Negligent (statutory — s.2(1) MA 1967)=Burden shifts to representor to prove reasonable grounds for belief; Innocent (s.2(2) MA 1967)=Neither fraudulent nor negligent; court may award damages in lieu of rescission; Common law negligent misstatement=Breach of duty of care in a 'special relationship' — Hedley Byrne v Heller [1964]

options.text: Made knowingly, without belief in its truth, or recklessly — *Derry v Peek* [1889]
Burden shifts to representor to prove reasonable grounds for belief
Neither fraudulent nor negligent; court may award damages in lieu of rescission
Breach of duty of care in a 'special relationship' — *Hedley Byrne v Heller* [1964]

feedback: Correctly matched. The key distinction between s.2(1) and Hedley Byrne is the burden of proof: s.2(1) reverses it; Hedley Byrne requires the claimant to prove a duty of care, breach, and reliance. s.2(2) is a judicial safety net for innocent misrepresentations.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: A management consultant tells a client that a target company 'generates annual profits of £2 million.' The consultant genuinely believes this, having been given false figures by the target's director. The client buys the company and discovers profits are only £500,000. The client sues the consultant under s.2(1) Misrepresentation Act 1967. What must the consultant prove to avoid liability?

options.text: That the target's director fraudulently deceived him
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under s.2(1), the consultant's defence is his own reasonable belief, not the source of his error. Even if the director deceived him, the consultant must still show he had reasonable grounds.

options.text: That he had reasonable grounds to believe, and did believe up to the contract, that the profits were £2 million
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.2(1) Misrepresentation Act 1967, the burden shifts to the representor to prove reasonable grounds for belief. The consultant must show he took reasonable steps to verify the figures — merely relying on the director's say-so may not be enough.

options.text: That the client should have verified the profits independently
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(1) does not impose a duty on the representee to verify. The representor bears the burden of proving reasonable grounds. Contributory negligence is not generally a defence to a s.2(1) claim (though controversial).

options.text: That the misrepresentation was innocent
options.feedback: Incorrect. If the consultant proves the misrepresentation was innocent (not negligent), he would fall under s.2(2) instead. Under s.2(1), the question is whether reasonable grounds existed — not whether the misrepresentation was innocent or not.

feedback: The s.2(1) reverse burden is a powerful tool for claimants. The consultant made a false statement of fact that induced the contract. To escape liability, he must do more than show he was misled by the director — he must prove he had reasonable grounds to believe the figures were true. If he simply passed on figures without verification, he is unlikely to succeed in that defence.

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law on types of misrepresentation under English law?

options.text: Under Derry v Peek [1889], fraud requires proof of knowledge, reckless disregard for truth, or absence of honest belief
options.feedback: Correct. *Derry v Peek* [1889] 14 App Cas 337: fraud encompasses knowledge, recklessness (careless whether true or false), and absence of honest belief. Honest belief, even if unreasonable, is not fraud.

options.text: Under s.2(1) Misrepresentation Act 1967, the burden of proof shifts to the representor to show reasonable grounds for belief
options.feedback: Correct. s.2(1) creates a reverse burden: the representor must positively prove reasonable grounds. This is the provision's key advantage over the tort of deceit.

options.text: Under s.2(2) Misrepresentation Act 1967, the court may award exemplary damages for innocent misrepresentation
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(2) provides for damages in lieu of rescission, not exemplary damages. The court uses its equitable discretion to award compensation instead of unwinding the contract.

options.text: Hedley Byrne v Heller [1964] liability requires a fiduciary relationship between the parties
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Hedley Byrne* requires a 'special relationship' — essentially, assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance — not a fiduciary relationship.

options.text: Innocent misrepresentation always entitles the representee to rescind the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Rescission is a discretionary remedy subject to bars: affirmation, lapse of time, third party rights, and impossibility of restitutio in integrum. Even where rescission is lost, s.2(2) may provide damages in lieu.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (b). *Derry v Peek* [1889] defines fraud; s.2(1) reverses the burden. The critical practical distinction: a claimant alleging fraud must prove dishonesty (a high bar); under s.2(1), the claimant need only prove a misrepresentation — the representor must then disprove negligence.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: In Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v __________ & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465, the House of Lords recognised a duty of care for negligent misstatement where a 'special relationship' exists between the parties.
feedback: Correct. *Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd* [1964] AC 465 — the bank was not liable because of an express disclaimer, but the House of Lords established that a duty of care can arise for negligent misstatements where the maker possesses special skill, assumes responsibility, and the recipient reasonably relies.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: Under s.2(____) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967, the court has discretion to award damages in lieu of rescission where the misrepresentation is neither fraudulent nor negligent.
feedback: Correct. Section 2(2) provides an equitable safety net. Where rescission has been lost (for example, due to affirmation or impossibility of restitutio in integrum) or would be disproportionate, the court may instead award damages.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule (Caveat Emptor):** There is no general duty of disclosure in English contract law — let the buyer beware. A party is not obliged to volunteer information that might affect the other's decision. Silence, standing alone, is not a misrepresentation. But there are critical exceptions.

**1. Changed Circumstances — With v O'Flanagan [1936] Ch 575:** A doctor selling his medical practice truthfully stated his takings were £2,000 per annum. Between that statement and completion, the doctor fell ill and the practice withered — takings collapsed to £5 per week. The Court of Appeal held there was a duty to disclose the change. A representation that is true when made but becomes false before the contract is concluded must be corrected. The duty is a continuing one.

**2. Half-Truths — Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21:** A vendor stated that tenants' notices to quit had been served on them. This was literally true — but the vendor failed to mention that the tenants had disputed those notices. A statement that is literally true but misleading because it omits material facts can amount to a misrepresentation. Telling half the truth can be as deceptive as telling a lie.

**3. Contracts Uberrimae Fidei (of Utmost Good Faith):** In certain contracts — classically insurance — there is a positive duty to disclose all material facts. Non-disclosure renders the contract voidable. The duty arises from the nature of the relationship, not from any positive statement.

**4. Fiduciary Relationships:** Where a fiduciary relationship exists (trustee-beneficiary, solicitor-client, agent-principal), there is a positive duty of disclosure. The fiduciary must disclose material information to the principal.

**In short:** Silence is not generally actionable, but (1) if a statement becomes false before completion (*With v O'Flanagan*), (2) if a half-truth creates a misleading impression (*Dimmock v Hallett*), or (3) if the contract or relationship imposes a duty of disclosure (insurance, fiduciary relationships), silence or omission can found a claim.
oscoaReferences: Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21

question: In With v __________ [1936] Ch 575, the Court of Appeal held that a representor must correct a statement that was true when made but becomes false before the contract is concluded.
feedback: Correct. *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] Ch 575 — the doctor's statement about his practice's takings (£2,000 pa) was true when made, but his failure to disclose the later collapse to £5 per week was a misrepresentation. The duty to correct arises by operation of law — it is a continuing duty.

question: Match each exception to the no-duty-of-disclosure rule with its correct description:
answer: Changed circumstances (With v O'Flanagan [1936])=Statement true when made but becomes false — duty to correct before completion; Half-truth (Dimmock v Hallett [1866])=Literally true statement that is misleading because material facts are omitted; Contracts uberrimae fidei=Positive duty to disclose all material facts (e.g. insurance contracts); Fiduciary relationships=Duty of disclosure arising from relationship of trust and confidence

options.text: Statement true when made but becomes false — duty to correct before completion
Literally true statement that is misleading because material facts are omitted
Positive duty to disclose all material facts (e.g. insurance contracts)
Duty of disclosure arising from relationship of trust and confidence

feedback: Well matched. These four exceptions carve significant inroads into the caveat emptor rule. The key to identifying the applicable exception is the source of the duty: continuing representation (*With v O'Flanagan*), misleading half-truth (*Dimmock v Hallett*), contractual duty (uberrimae fidei), or relational duty (fiduciary).
oscoaReferences: Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21

question: A restaurant owner tells a buyer in March: 'The restaurant averages 200 covers per night.' This was true when said. By completion in August, a food hygiene scandal has caused covers to drop to 30 per night. The owner says nothing about the drop. The buyer completes. Applying *With v O'Flanagan* [1936], what is the legal position?

options.text: The owner had no duty to speak — the statement was true when made and silence is not a misrepresentation
options.feedback: Incorrect. *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] is directly on point: a statement true when made but rendered false by subsequent events creates a continuing duty to correct.

options.text: The owner had a duty to correct the statement before completion — a representation true when made but rendered false by subsequent events must be corrected
options.feedback: Correct. Under *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] Ch 575, a representor whose statement was true when made but becomes false before the contract is concluded is under a duty to disclose the change. Silence in this context is a misrepresentation.

options.text: The buyer's remedy is limited to the tort of deceit
options.feedback: Incorrect. The buyer has remedies under the Misrepresentation Act 1967 and may also have a claim under s.2(1) or s.2(2), depending on the owner's state of mind. Deceit is not the only route.

options.text: The owner is only liable if the buyer specifically asked about current covers before completion
options.feedback: Incorrect. *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] imposes a positive duty to correct — it does not depend on the representee asking questions. The duty arises automatically when the earlier representation becomes false.

feedback: The *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] principle is directly applicable. The statement about 200 covers was a continuing representation. When covers collapsed to 30 before completion, the owner was obliged to disclose the change. By remaining silent, the owner misrepresented the restaurant's current trading position. The buyer can claim.

question: Which TWO of the following are recognised exceptions to the general rule that silence does not amount to a misrepresentation?

options.text: A statement true when made becomes false before the contract is concluded (With v O'Flanagan [1936])
options.feedback: Correct. *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] Ch 575 establishes that a continuing representation must be corrected if circumstances change before completion.

options.text: A vendor fails to disclose a defect that would reduce the property's market value
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is the caveat emptor rule in action. Mere non-disclosure of a defect, without a positive representation or a duty of disclosure, is not a misrepresentation.

options.text: A statement is literally true but misleading because it omits material facts (Dimmock v Hallett [1866])
options.feedback: Correct. *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21 — telling half the truth can amount to a misrepresentation where the omitted facts make the statement misleading.

options.text: A party fails to disclose their negotiating strategy or bottom-line price
options.feedback: Incorrect. Parties are free to keep their negotiating positions confidential. There is no duty to disclose how much one is willing to pay or accept.

options.text: A seller remains silent about a market change that makes the contract less advantageous to the buyer
options.feedback: Incorrect. General market conditions or changes in value do not, without more, impose a duty of disclosure. Caveat emptor applies to fluctuations the buyer could discover themselves.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The two major inroads on caveat emptor are changed circumstances (*With v O'Flanagan* [1936]) and half-truths (*Dimmock v Hallett* [1866]). Simple non-disclosure of a defect or market shift, without more, does not amount to a misrepresentation.
oscoaReferences: Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21

question: In _________ v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21, a vendor's statement that tenants' notices to quit had been served was misleading because the tenants had disputed those notices — a literal truth can be a misrepresentation if it gives a false impression.
feedback: Correct. *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21 — the vendor's failure to disclose the tenants' challenge rendered the literally true statement misleading. A half-truth can be as deceptive as a falsehood.
oscoaReferences: Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21

question: A landlord tells a prospective tenant: 'The property has been fully rewired and complies with all building regulations.' This is literally true — the rewiring was completed and a certificate issued. However, the landlord does not mention that the rewiring was necessitated by a severe fire that caused structural damage still being assessed. The tenant moves in and discovers the fire damage. Applying *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866]:

options.text: No misrepresentation — the statement was literally true and the fire damage is a separate issue from the rewiring
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866] establishes that a literally true statement can be misleading by omission. The cause of the rewiring (fire) and the ongoing structural damage are material facts that make the statement misleading.

options.text: Yes — a statement that is literally true but misleading by omission can amount to a misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21, telling half the truth can be a misrepresentation. The landlord's statement created a misleading impression by omitting the material fact of fire damage.

options.text: No — the fire damage is a defect in the property, and the tenant should have inspected
options.feedback: Incorrect. While caveat emptor applies to defects, this is a positive representation about the property's condition that was misleading by omission — that goes beyond mere non-disclosure.

options.text: Yes — but only if the tenant specifically asked whether the property had suffered fire damage
options.feedback: Incorrect. The duty to avoid half-truths applies regardless of whether questions were asked. A positive statement that is misleading does not become actionable only if the representee makes further inquiry.

feedback: The *Dimmock v Hallett* [1866] principle applies: a literally true statement can be a misrepresentation where it conveys a misleading impression by omitting material facts. The landlord's statement about rewiring was true, but the omission of the fire and structural damage created a false picture of the property's condition and history.
oscoaReferences: Dimmock v Hallett [1866] LR 2 Ch App 21

question: Contracts __________ fidei, such as insurance contracts, are a recognised exception to the rule that there is no duty of disclosure in English contract law.
feedback: Correct. Contracts uberrimae fidei (of utmost good faith) require each party to disclose all material facts. Insurance is the classic example — the insured must disclose facts that would influence a prudent insurer. Non-disclosure renders the contract voidable.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] A tech founder tells an angel investor that the company 'holds three granted patents protecting its core technology.' The investor puts in £200,000, partly because of the patents and partly because he 'likes the team.' In truth, the company only has pending applications — no patents have been granted. The investor admits the team was the main factor but the patents 'gave me confidence.' Applying *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885]:

options.text: The investor cannot claim because he admits the team was the main factor — the patents were not decisive
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Edgington* [1885] does not require the misrepresentation to be the main factor. It need only be 'a' material inducement.

options.text: The investor can claim — the patent statement was 'a' material inducement, even if not the main reason for investing
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] 29 Ch D 459, the misrepresentation need only be a material factor. The investor's mixed motives (patents and team) do not defeat the claim.

options.text: The investor cannot claim because pending applications are close enough to granted patents
options.feedback: Incorrect. A pending application and a granted patent are fundamentally different — an application may be refused. The statement was false: the company did not hold granted patents.

options.text: The investor cannot claim because he should have checked the patent register himself
options.feedback: Incorrect. A representee is not required to verify a representation (*Redgrave v Hurd* [1881]). Reliance, not due diligence, is the test.

feedback: This consolidates Block A: the *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] principle. The investor's mixed motives mirror the original Edgington facts — an investor partly induced by a false prospectus and partly by his own mistaken belief. The false patent statement was a material inducement. The claim succeeds.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] The case of Derry v __________ [1889] 14 App Cas 337 provides the classic definition of fraudulent misrepresentation.
feedback: Correct. *Derry v Peek* [1889] 14 App Cas 337 established that fraud requires knowledge, absence of honest belief, or recklessness as to truth. Honest belief, however unreasonable, is not fraud. This consolidates the core distinction between fraud and negligence.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] In January, a shop owner tells a buyer: 'Turnover is £10,000 per week — here are the accounts.' This was true. By completion in July, a competitor has opened and turnover has dropped to £2,000 per week. The seller stays silent. Applying *With v O'Flanagan* [1936]:

options.text: The seller had a continuing duty to correct the representation — the silence was a misrepresentation
options.feedback: Correct. Under *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] Ch 575, a representation true when made but rendered false before completion must be corrected. The seller's silence was a misrepresentation.

options.text: The seller had no duty — the statement was true when made and the buyer could have asked for updated accounts
options.feedback: Incorrect. *With v O'Flanagan* imposes a positive duty to correct — it is not discharged by the possibility that the buyer could have asked questions.

options.text: The seller is liable only if the buyer proves the seller knew the competitor was opening
options.feedback: Incorrect. The duty under *With v O'Flanagan* arises regardless of fault — it is strict. The seller need not be aware the earlier statement is now false. What matters is that objectively, the representation has become false.

options.text: The seller is not liable because a change in market conditions is different from the personal illness in the original case
options.feedback: Incorrect. The principle is not limited to personal circumstances. *With v O'Flanagan* applies whenever a continuing representation becomes false before completion, whatever the cause.

feedback: This consolidates Block C: the *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] principle. The statement about turnover was a continuing representation. When turnover collapsed before completion, the seller was under a positive duty to correct it. The cause of the change (competition, illness, scandal) does not matter — the duty is strict.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Which TWO of the following correctly summarise key principles of misrepresentation under English law?

options.text: A misrepresentation must be the sole reason for entering the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] — a misrepresentation need only be 'a' material inducement. Mixed motives do not defeat the claim.

options.text: Where a statement true when made becomes false before completion, the representor has a continuing duty to correct it (With v O'Flanagan [1936])
options.feedback: Correct. *With v O'Flanagan* [1936] Ch 575 — the duty to correct arises automatically; it is not dependent on the representee's inquiries.

options.text: Under s.2(1) Misrepresentation Act 1967, the burden shifts to the representor to prove reasonable grounds for believing the statement was true
options.feedback: Correct. s.2(1) reverses the burden of proof. The representee proves a misrepresentation; the representor must then prove they had reasonable grounds to believe it was true.

options.text: A statement of opinion can never, under any circumstances, be an actionable misrepresentation
options.feedback: Incorrect. An opinion can be actionable if the representor did not genuinely hold it (*Edgington* — statement of intention not genuinely held; *Smith v Land & House Property Corp* [1884] — opinion implying false facts).

options.text: There is a general duty of disclosure in all pre-contractual negotiations under English law
options.feedback: Incorrect. The general rule is caveat emptor — no duty of disclosure. Exceptions exist for uberrimae fidei contracts, fiduciary relationships, changed circumstances, and half-truths, but these are exceptions, not the rule.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). These span all three blocks: (b) from Block C (*With v O'Flanagan*), and (c) from Block B (s.2(1) MA 1967). The wrong answers reinforce key points: mixed motives do not defeat a claim (Block A), opinions can be actionable, and caveat emptor is the default rule (Block C).

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] The case of Edgington v __________ [1885] 29 Ch D 459 established that a misrepresentation need only be 'a' material inducement — the representee need not have relied on it exclusively.
feedback: Correct. *Edgington v Fitzmaurice* [1885] 29 Ch D 459 is the leading authority on mixed motives. The principle is fundamental to understanding reliance in misrepresentation — perfect parity with *Derry v Peek* defines the boundaries between fraud, negligence, and innocent misstatement.
